does: This paper presents Garcon, a software infrastructure and tooling suite designed to enable interpretability research on large-scale neural language models. The authors describe the practical systems, computational infrastructure, and implementation details necessary to apply mechanistic interpretability techniques—such as activation patching, causal intervention, circuit analysis, and logit lens—to models with billions of parameters. Garcon provides researchers with accessible abstractions for instrumenting models, extracting and manipulating internal representations, and conducting systematic experiments on model internals, addressing the engineering challenges that arise when scaling interpretability methods from toy models to production-scale systems.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of mechanistic interpretability research that seeks to understand neural networks by probing internal mechanisms rather than treating them as black boxes. It extends prior work on techniques like circuit discovery, activation patching, and feature attribution that have been developed primarily on smaller models or in research settings with limited infrastructure. Garcon responds to the practical bottleneck identified in interpretability research: while conceptual methods have advanced, the engineering infrastructure to apply them systematically to large models remains scattered and difficult. The tooling connects to broader efforts in neural network analysis and the push toward interpretable machine learning in safety-critical applications.
enables: By providing accessible infrastructure for large-model interpretability, Garcon enables researchers to conduct systematic mechanistic studies at scale, potentially uncovering how emergent behaviors arise in large language models. The tooling can support hypothesis-driven investigation of model capabilities, failure modes, and alignment properties—critical for understanding and potentially controlling model behavior. Future work can build on Garcon to develop automated circuit discovery, scalable feature analysis, and systematic evaluation of interpretability techniques across model families and scales, moving interpretability research from isolated studies toward standardized, reproducible science applicable to deployed systems.
